A Page Built Around One Tall Image

By Test Harness

This essay exists mainly to host the tall figure below, which loads slowly on purpose so the reader must re-measure once it arrives. The surrounding prose is here so the page reads as a genuine article rather than a bare image, which is what a real reading tool would encounter in the wild.

Forum threads, photo essays, and gallery posts frequently put a single large image at the very top of the content, above any substantial text. When that image has not yet downloaded, the browser reports its height as zero, and a layout engine that measures the page exactly once will badly under-count how much vertical space the article truly needs.

If pagination is only measured before the image loads, everything above collapses into a single short column and the rest of the article becomes an unreachable blank spread. Re-measuring after the image arrives restores the full set of pages so the reader can flip all the way to the end.

The fix is to watch each still-loading image and re-run the layout pass once it settles, keeping the reader on its current spread while quietly extending the reachable range of pages behind the scenes. The same applies when a web font swaps in and reflows the text after the first measurement.
